name: Sync Window
description: A set of latency-adjusted judgment bands that classify inputs as perfect, good, late, early, missed, or ignored relative to charted beat timestamps.
design_notes: Sync windows are the fairness contract in rhythm games. The implementation should define exact window boundaries, input consumption rules, combo-break rules, calibration offsets, replay behavior, and how dropped frames affect visual feedback without changing scoring.
design_purpose: Translate small timing differences into readable judgment tiers.
Give designers a structured way to tune difficulty and score generosity.
Support calibration and accessibility assists without rewriting chart data.
player_fantasy: The player feels their precision is judged consistently and understands why an input was early, perfect, late, or missed.

parameters.name: timing_window_id
parameters.description: Named judgment profile applied to the current song or mode.

parameters.name: perfect_window_ms
parameters.description: Maximum absolute offset for the highest judgment tier.

parameters.name: good_window_ms
parameters.description: Maximum absolute offset for an acceptable successful hit.

parameters.name: miss_window_ms
parameters.description: Boundary after which an unhit note becomes a miss.

parameters.name: input_latency_offset_ms
parameters.description: Player or device calibration offset applied before classification.

parameters.name: hit_judgment_id
parameters.description: Result assigned to a note or beat input after evaluation.

parameters.name: combo_break_rule_id
parameters.description: Rule that determines which judgments reset combo state.

edge_cases: Two notes are close enough that one input could fall inside both judgment windows.
A held note begins with a perfect hit but releases outside the allowed end band.
Calibration changes while a note is active and should apply only after the current chart segment.
A pause occurs after a note appears but before its miss boundary.
Replay verification runs on a device with different display refresh and audio latency.
common_bugs: Judgment windows are checked in a non-deterministic note order and consume inputs inconsistently.
Miss cleanup uses render position instead of audio-clock position.
Early and late offsets are displayed with reversed signs.
Assist mode widens visual feedback but not actual hit_judgment_id calculation.
Combo break logic reads score tier text instead of the structured combo_break_rule_id.
balancing_notes: Window sizes should match song density, input device, and intended rank difficulty.
Perfect windows can be strict if good windows preserve playability and feedback is accurate.
Ranked modes may need fixed calibration policy while casual modes can be more forgiving.
accessibility_notes: Offer adjustable timing windows or assist profiles separate from audiovisual latency calibration.
Show early and late feedback in text or clear symbols, not only color.
Support practice views that display input offset history without requiring external tools.

implementation_notes.unity: Store timing_window_id profiles as data and evaluate input_event_timestamp_ms against audio-clock beat timestamps after one calibration offset. Sort candidate notes by absolute offset and consume an input only once to keep replay scoring deterministic.
implementation_notes.godot: Use a rhythm judgment node that receives timestamped input events and chart note timestamps. Keep miss cleanup tied to audio_playhead_ms, and emit structured hit_judgment_id values for UI, combo, and scoring.
implementation_notes.unreal: A rhythm subsystem should own window profiles, calibration, and note-consumption order. Use fixed data ordering for replay and separate visual hit effects from authoritative judgment state.
implementation_notes.web: Use Web Audio currentTime plus event timestamps to compute offsets. Store judgment profiles in chart metadata, and process note hits in a deterministic sorted array rather than relying on DOM or render positions.

relationships.reason: Sync windows depend on the rhythm timing clock, calibration offset, and audio playhead.
Beat matching uses sync windows to classify early, perfect, late, or missed inputs.
Assist settings can widen judgment windows or add calibration profiles for latency tolerance.

example_games.title: Guitar Hero
example_games.note: Uses timing windows to judge note hits against charted music.

example_games.title: osu!
example_games.note: Uses timing judgments and offset feedback as part of scoring.
